channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: All eyes on Orion’s heat shield: Artemis 2 astronauts will hit Earth's atmosphere at nearly 24,000 mph on April 10 - Space
item.description:
1. All eyes on Orion’s heat shield: Artemis 2 astronauts will hit Earth's atmosphere at nearly 24,000 mph on April 10  Space
  2. A cracked heat shield rattled NASA after Artemis I. Now, Artemis II will put the fix to the test  Los Angeles Times
  3. How to see astronauts off California’s coast in historic Artemis II splash down Friday  New York Post
  4. NASA Prepares for Artemis II Splashdown After Historic Moon Flyby  The New York Times
  5. Instead Of An Aircraft Carrier, This Ship Will Recover The Orion Spacecraft  Forbes

item.description:
1. Artemis II Astronauts Witnessed 6 Meteorites Colliding With the Moon  WIRED

  3. NASA's Artemis II Crew Saw Meteorites Striking The Moon in Real Time  ScienceAlert
  4. NASA Artemis II astronauts prepare to end moon mission in 'fireball' re-entry  Reuters
  5. Artemis II crew emotional as mission prepares for reentry  dw.com

item.description:

  5. Astronauts Dedicate Moon Crater to Carroll Wiseman, Wife of NASA Commander  The New York Times

item.title: Scientists say the world’s oldest octopus fossil isn’t an octopus after all - AP News
item.description:
1. Scientists say the world’s oldest octopus fossil isn’t an octopus after all  AP News
  2. "Weird blob" creature thought to be the world's oldest octopus isn't an octopus after all. Here's what scientists found.  CBS News
  3. This Fossil Once Rewrote Everything We Knew About Octopuses. Turns Out, It's Not An Octopus At All  IFLScience
  4. Museum display found to be entirely new species after 26 years: 'Difficult to decipher'  Yahoo News Australia
  5. 'Oldest octopus' fossil is no octopus at all, scans reveal  Phys.org

item.title: The lengths male octopuses go to protect the arm they need to mate - Phys.org
item.description:
1. The lengths male octopuses go to protect the arm they need to mate  Phys.org
  2. Male octopus has ‘sex arm’ that can mate in the dark  National Geographic
  3. How octopuses have sex without even seeing each other  Euronews.com
  4. The One Arm an Octopus Will Never Risk in a Fight—Its Sex Arm  ZME Science
  5. A Harvard researcher started studying how octopuses use receptors in their arms to “taste by touch” while hunting prey and exploring. In doing so, his team stumbled on answers to the birds and the bees of cephalopods getting busy. https://wapo.st/4dNeHTR  facebook.com

item.title: Daylight fireball seen from New Jersey, New York, and Pennsylvania - The Watchers - Watching the world evolve and transform
item.description:

item.source: The Watchers - Watching the world evolve and transform

item.title: Is the next 'great comet' coming? See Comet PanSTARRS brighten in the night sky this week. - Live Science
item.description:

item.title: This Company Is Launching 4,000 Orbiting ‘Sky Mirrors’ to Beam Sunlight Down to Earth After Dark. Scientists Want It Stopped Now - Indian Defence Review
item.description:
1. This Company Is Launching 4,000 Orbiting ‘Sky Mirrors’ to Beam Sunlight Down to Earth After Dark. Scientists Want It Stopped Now  Indian Defence Review

  3. Wild plan to control sunlight by installing 50,000 mirrors in space could wreak havoc on Earth, experts warn: ‘Major adverse health consequences’  New York Post
  4. Satellite mirrors could threaten night skies and ecosystems, scientists warn  Anadolu Ajansı
  5. SpaceX satellites would outshine the stars  The Narwhal

item.title: Satellite images reveal hidden cause of melting glaciers in Greenland - BBC Wildlife Magazine
item.description:
